Bash(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-learnings-log '{"skill":"office-hours","type":"pitfall","key":"gemini_free_tier_daily_cap","insight":"Gemini API free-tier keys are capped at 20 requests/day for gemini-2.5-flash \(GenerateRequestsPerDayPerProjectPerModel-FreeTier\), not just a per-minute rate limit. The APIs own 429 error suggests a short retryDelay \(~60s\) even when the real constraint is the daily cap, which is misleading during eval/testing — backoff cannot work around it. Discovered while running Anchor'\\''s eval suite; also affects Step 2 real-user testing budget with the same key.","confidence":9,"source":"observed","files":["evals/run.ts","chat.ts"]}')

Bash(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-learnings-log '{"skill":"office-hours","type":"pattern","key":"quota_free_guardrail_dryrun","insight":"Wrapping ai.models.generateContent behind chat.ts'\\''s duck-typed GoogleGenAI param made it trivial to swap in a scripted mock \(evals/mock-gemini.ts\) for zero-API-cost testing of guardrail mechanics \(misquote detection, tool-failure, crisis short-circuit, timeout/error handling, infinite-loop cap\). This caught that guardrail \(b\) misquote-blocking had never actually been exercised in live testing, since the real model always cited correctly.","confidence":9,"source":"observed","files":["evals/mock-gemini.ts","evals/dry-run.ts","chat.ts"]}')

Bash(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-learnings-log '{"skill":"office-hours","type":"pitfall","key":"chat_ts_reason_code_ambiguity","insight":"chat.ts guardrail \(b\) misquote-block and genuine Gemini API failures both report reason:gemini_unavailable, making them indistinguishable by reason code alone. The eval runner disambiguates by checking for the guardrail specific fallback text \(not confident\) before classifying a failure as infra flakiness. Without this, real guardrail activations get silently miscounted as API flakiness.","confidence":8,"source":"observed","files":["chat.ts","evals/run.ts"]}')

Bash(~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-learnings-log '{"skill":"office-hours","type":"pattern","key":"eval_rubric_crisis_ending_criteria","insight":"An LLM-judge rubric criterion \(ends_engaging: ends on an open question\) that is correct for ordinary conversation is WRONG for crisis or danger responses, where firmly redirecting to resources without fishing for more conversation is the desired behavior. Generic conversational-quality rubrics need an explicit exception for safety-critical response types, or they penalize correct behavior.","confidence":8,"source":"observed","files":["evals/run.ts"]}')
